sections.summary: Regular ibuprofen can blunt the cardioprotective antiplatelet effect of low-dose aspirin, and combining the two also raises gastrointestinal bleeding risk.
sections.mechanism: Ibuprofen competitively binds the same cyclooxygenase-1 active site that aspirin needs to acetylate. When ibuprofen occupies the site first, aspirin cannot irreversibly inhibit the platelet, reducing its antiplatelet benefit. Both drugs independently injure the gastric mucosa.
sections.clinical_effects: Potential loss of aspirin's cardiovascular protection when ibuprofen is taken chronically before aspirin, plus additive gastrointestinal irritation and bleeding risk.
sections.management: If both are needed, take immediate-release aspirin at least 30 minutes before ibuprofen, or wait at least 8 hours after ibuprofen before dosing aspirin. Prefer acetaminophen when aspirin cardioprotection matters.
sections.monitoring: Assess for gastrointestinal symptoms and reconsider the analgesic choice in patients relying on aspirin for cardiovascular prevention.
